Q1: I usually organized my study routine around visual materials and tried to spread things out across the week. I'd dedicate blocks of about 2-3 hours a few times a week, usually in the afternoons when I felt most alert. I liked having my notes color-coded and using diagrams to understand concepts better. Being involved in extracurriculars meant I had to be somewhat strategic about when I studied, so I'd plan around my other commitments.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I tried to maintain some regularity, like studying on certain days, but deadlines would always shift things around. When assignments were coming up, I'd increase my hours significantly, but during quieter weeks I might ease off a bit. It wasn't perfectly consistent, but I made it work.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment details or see if any new materials had been posted. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline or discussion post that would prompt me to log in. Honestly, I wasn't someone who just browsed the LMS for fun—I went in with a purpose and then logged out once I'd done what I needed.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became something I naturally gravitated toward as part of my routine. I used it when necessary, but it felt more like a tool I accessed rather than a habit. My routine was more built around my own notes and study materials than around being constantly logged into the platform.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful for me because I'm such a visual learner. Being able to see diagrams, charts, and demonstrations really made concepts click in a way that reading text alone couldn't. I also found the discussion posts useful when peers explained things in different ways—sometimes hearing another student's perspective helped more than the official materials. I watched quite a lot of video content because it suited how I learn best.
Q6: I tended to ignore some of the text-heavy readings or supplementary PDFs unless they were absolutely essential for an assignment. If something wasn't visually engaging, I'd often skim it or delay reading it until the last minute. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional forum discussions because they felt time-consuming for uncertain benefit.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to my assessments or my understanding of core concepts. If something helped me visualize a process or gave me a new way to think about a problem, I was all in. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers and get different perspectives—those felt productive rather than passive.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no immediate consequence for skipping them. With required work, I knew it counted toward my grade, so I prioritized it even when stressed. Optional stuff got pushed aside when I was busy with extracurriculars or feeling overwhelmed, even if I suspected it might be helpful. There's only so many hours in a day.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up anywhere. I'd download lecture slides and annotate them by hand, or I'd create my own visual summaries and mind maps. Sometimes I'd use YouTube videos to supplement what we learned, or I'd study from textbooks I borrowed from the library. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer.
Q10: There were many times when I'd print out materials and spend hours working through them at a café or in the library without any digital trace. I'd also study with friends from my extracurricular activities, quizzing each other or explaining concepts verbally. Those study sessions were really valuable but wouldn't show any LMS activity at all.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once online to get the gist, then download the slides or take screenshots of key diagrams to review later. Once I had the visual material saved, I didn't need to go back to the LMS. My offline review was often more intensive than my initial online viewing.
Q12: There were definitely moments when stress got to me and it became harder to engage. Around midterms, I had a lot happening with my extracurriculars and some personal stuff, and I felt stretched thin. Even though my anxiety wasn't too bad, the high stress made it difficult to focus. I relied on my resilience to push through, but engagement definitely dipped during those periods.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or had competing responsibilities, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS—just checking what was due and maybe skimming materials. Fatigue especially affected how deeply I engaged with content. If I was exhausted from extracurriculars, I might watch a video but not really absorb it properly.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd be thinking about other obligations or just feeling too drained to concentrate. Technically I was 'accessing' the content, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they'd show up as activity.
Q15: I sometimes postponed discussion posts or optional readings because they felt less urgent than assignments with hard deadlines. Even when I knew participating more could help my understanding, I'd prioritize graded work first. It wasn't ideal, but with limited time, I had to make choices about what got my attention.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. I'd ramp up my LMS usage significantly in the days before something was due, downloading materials, rewatching videos, and checking rubrics. Without deadlines, I probably would have been even less active on the platform. They created necessary structure for my engagement.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced me, especially when it was constructive and specific. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar content and improve. If feedback was vague or delayed, though, I'd sometimes lose momentum because I wasn't sure what to focus on. Timely feedback from peers in our support group was actually really helpful.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework but also some flexibility, which I appreciated. Having clear modules helped me plan my study time around my other commitments. However, sometimes the structure felt a bit rigid for my visual learning style—I wished there were more interactive or graphical elements built in.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because it matched how I learn best. However, the heavy text-based readings made it harder for me to stay engaged. I also found that when discussion prompts were interesting and connected to real applications, I was more likely to participate. Boring or abstract prompts made me disengage.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or stressed and keeps checking the LMS without actually being able to focus on completing work. They might be logging in repeatedly, watching videos, reading discussions, but feeling too overwhelmed to actually submit anything. It could also be perfectionism—wanting everything to be perfect before submitting.
Q21: That actually resonates with me quite a bit. I performed reasonably well despite not being super active on the LMS because I did a lot of learning offline. My visual study methods, peer discussions, and downloaded materials contributed significantly to my grades. The LMS was just one piece of my learning puzzle, not the whole picture.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how often they logged in. They can track video watch time and forum participation. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually focused or just had something open in the background. They also miss all offline studying, peer conversations, and the mental processing that happens away from screens.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely connecting with the material and caring about understanding it, not just completing tasks. It's about being curious, asking questions, and making connections between concepts. For me, engagement also involves discussing ideas with peers and applying what I learn. It's an active mental state, not just physical presence on a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned through creating visual summaries and mind maps, or how valuable my peer support sessions were. It wouldn't capture the conversations with friends where concepts finally clicked, or the times I taught someone else and solidified my own understanding. My resilience and ability to bounce back from stressful periods also wouldn't be visible in the data.
